meta.name: Garlic Toum (Lebanese Garlic Sauce)
meta.subtitle: Fluffy, intense garlic emulsion for shawarma, grilled meats, and roasted vegetables

source.name: SSA Canonical Catalogs
notes: Classic toum is an emulsified garlic + oil sauce (no eggs, no dairy).
This version includes SSA-friendly checkpoints for emulsion success.
Use a food processor for reliability; mortar-and-pestle is optional but slower.

summary: Traditional toum: a powerful, fluffy garlic sauce made by emulsifying garlic with neutral oil and lemon juice. No eggs, no dairy—just technique.

yield.notes: Makes about 1.5–2 cups (varies with oil absorption and garlic size).

optional.label: Immersion blender + tall cup
optional.notes: Works but is less forgiving than a processor for classic toum. Add oil extra slowly.

optional.label: Mortar and pestle
optional.notes: Traditional method; more time and arm work. Emulsion is possible with patience.

notes: Contains garlic (allium).
If using mustard variation, check mustard sensitivity.

ingredients.label: Garlic cloves, peeled
ingredients.notes: About 18–24 cloves, depending on size. Fresh, firm garlic emulsifies best.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt
ingredients.notes: Salt helps break down garlic into a paste and supports emulsion.

ingredients.label: Fresh lemon juice
ingredients.notes: About 2–3 lemons. Add gradually; too much at once can break the emulsion.

ingredients.label: Neutral oil (grapeseed, sunflower, avocado, or canola)
ingredients.notes: Avoid strongly flavored olive oil for classic toum; use neutral for clean garlic punch.

ingredients.label: Ice water
ingredients.notes: Helps keep the emulsion cool and stable if the processor warms up.

ingredients.label: Dijon mustard (optional stabilizer)
ingredients.notes: Not traditional but can help beginners stabilize the emulsion.

steps.title: Prep for a stable emulsion
instructions: Make sure your food processor bowl and blade are completely clean and dry.
Use peeled garlic cloves with any green sprouts removed (sprouts can taste bitter).
Have oil and lemon juice measured and ready—toum moves quickly once emulsifying starts.
checks: Processor bowl is dry and oil/lemon are measured.

steps.title: Make a smooth garlic paste
instructions: Add garlic and salt to the food processor.
Process 60–90 seconds, stopping to scrape down the sides 2–3 times.
Goal: a very smooth paste (like wet sand to creamy paste). The smoother the paste, the fluffier the toum.
checks: Garlic is finely processed with no large chunks.

steps.title: Begin emulsifying (oil in a very thin stream)
instructions: With the processor running, start adding oil in a *very thin* steady stream (almost drops at first).
After about 1/4 cup oil, the mixture should start turning pale and thick.
If it looks glossy and loose, slow down the oil stream and keep processing.
checks: Mixture becomes pale and begins to thicken after initial oil.

steps.title: Alternate oil and lemon juice
instructions: Continue adding oil slowly, then drizzle in a small splash of lemon juice.
Keep alternating: oil (thin stream) → small lemon splash → oil → lemon.
If the processor bowl warms up, add 1 tbsp ice water to cool and stabilize (optional).
checks: Sauce is thick, fluffy, and holds soft peaks.

steps.title: Finish and adjust thickness
instructions: Once all oil is incorporated, add remaining lemon juice gradually to taste and brighten the sauce.
If too thick: add 1 tsp ice water at a time and pulse until smooth.
If too sharp: add a little more oil to mellow (slow drizzle).
Taste and adjust salt if needed.
checks: Toum is white, fluffy, and spreadable (not runny).

steps.title: If the emulsion breaks (rescue method)
instructions: If the mixture turns runny or looks separated: stop adding oil immediately.
In a clean bowl, whisk 1 tbsp lemon juice (or 1 tbsp water) with 1–2 tsp of the broken mixture until it thickens slightly.
With the processor running, slowly stream the broken mixture into the new base to re-emulsify.
Then resume adding oil *very slowly* if needed.
checks: Separated mixture becomes thick again as it re-emulsifies.

steps.title: Chill to set flavor
instructions: Transfer toum to a clean jar/container and refrigerate at least 30 minutes.
Flavor mellows and texture sets as it chills.

notes: sharp
pungent
creamy
bright
clean
Use sparingly in wraps—toum is potent.
Thin with a splash of water + lemon for a drizzle sauce.

fridge.notes: Store cold in a sealed container. Use clean utensils to avoid contamination.

freezer.notes: Freezing can cause separation and graininess.

notes: Because this is a raw-garlic sauce, keep refrigerated and do not leave at room temperature for extended periods.
Discard if it smells off, develops mold, or becomes unusually watery.

batchNotes: Double batch is efficient—toum keeps about a week and supports multiple shawarma meals.
For milder toum: reduce garlic to 3/4 cup and keep oil/lemon similar, adjusting to taste.

notes: Key success factor: oil must be added slowly at the beginning.
If it breaks, the rescue method works—don’t throw it out.
Neutral oil produces classic flavor; olive oil makes it heavier and more bitter.
Add 1 tsp Dijon mustard after processing the garlic paste.
Proceed with emulsion as written; mustard can help stability and reduce break risk.
Increase lemon juice to 1/3 cup, adding gradually at the end.
Balance by adding 1–2 tbsp additional oil if needed.
Replace half the raw garlic with roasted garlic cloves.
Start with slightly less oil; roasted garlic can thicken faster. Adjust oil/lemon to taste.
